sections.header: Verses 1–9 — “You Are the Most Excellent of Men”

heb.paragraph: "Your throne (kisʾăk̅ā), O God, will last for ever and ever" (v.6). The most debated verse: is the king addressed AS God, or is "God" a parenthetical (your throne, O God, is forever)? Hebrews 1:8–9 applies it directly to Christ: "But about the Son he says: ‘Your throne, O God, will last for ever.’" A royal wedding psalm that becomes messianic: the king is anointed with the oil of joy (v.7), his robes are fragrant (v.8), the queen stands at his right hand in gold of Ophir (v.9).

notes.note: "You are the most excellent of men and your lips have been anointed with grace." The ideal king: beautiful in speech, gracious in character.

"Your throne, O God, will last for ever. You love righteousness and hate wickedness; therefore God, your God, has set you above your companions by anointing you with the oil of joy." Hebrews 1:8–9 applies this to Christ’s superiority over angels.

refs.note: The author of Hebrews applies verses 6-7 ('Your throne, O God, will last forever') directly to Christ as evidence of divine sonship.
The rider on the white horse with a robe dipped in blood fulfills the psalm's warrior-king imagery.
The parable of the wedding banquet draws on the royal wedding tradition the psalm celebrates.

hist.context: Psalm 45 is a royal wedding song (Hebrew: shir yedidot, 'song of loves') celebrating a king's marriage. The bride appears to be a foreign princess (v.10: 'forget your people and your father's house'). Some connect it to Solomon's marriage to Pharaoh's daughter or Ahab's to Jezebel. The psalm's attribution to Korah and its use of 'Shoshannim' (lilies) in the superscription link it to other love poetry.

sections.header: Verses 10–17 — “The King Is Enthralled by Your Beauty”

heb.paragraph: "Listen, daughter, and pay attention: forget your people and your father’s house. Let the king be enthralled by your beauty" (vv.10–11). The bride is counselled to leave her old identity and enter the king’s household. The allegory: the church leaves the world to be enthralled by Christ. "The princess is all glorious within her chamber; her gown is interwoven with gold" (v.13). The psalm closes with a promise of lasting memory: "I will perpetuate your memory through all generations" (v.17).

notes.note: "Forget your people and your father’s house." The bride’s journey: leave the old, enter the new. The language echoes Abraham’s call (Gen 12:1) and Ruth’s commitment (Ruth 1:16).
"The princess is all glorious within." Inner glory, outer gold. The psalm ends with a promise: the king’s name will be remembered forever. Not earthly fame but eternal memory.

refs.note: Paul's bride-of-Christ ecclesiology transforms the psalm's royal marriage into a paradigm for Christ and the church.
The new Jerusalem coming down as a bride adorned for her husband completes the wedding imagery.
Paul presenting the church as a pure virgin to Christ draws on the bridal tradition.

hist.context: The address to the queen (v.9) uses the rare term shegal, possibly a foreign loanword, suggesting an international marriage alliance. The promise that the king's sons will be princes throughout the earth (v.16) extends the messianic reading. Jewish interpretation saw this as the marriage of Messiah to Israel; Christian interpretation transferred it to Christ and the Church.

ppl.name: The King (Messianic Bridegroom)
ppl.role: Royal figure addressed with divine language (vv.2-9)
ppl.text: The king is described in extraordinary terms: 'the most excellent of men,' with lips 'anointed with grace' (v.2), riding forth 'in the cause of truth, humility and justice' (v.4). Most remarkably, verse 6 declares 'Your throne, O God, will last for ever and ever' — either addressing the king as elohim or calling his throne God's. Hebrews 1:8-9 applies this directly to Christ, making it a cornerstone of NT Christology. The king is anointed with 'the oil of joy' above his companions (v.7).

ppl.name: The Royal Bride
ppl.role: The queen adorned in gold, called to leave her people (vv.10-15)
ppl.text: The bride is told 'forget your people and your father's house' (v.10) — language echoing Abraham's call (Gen 12:1) and Ruth's commitment (Ruth 1:16). She is 'all glorious within' (v.13) and led to the king 'in embroidered garments' (v.14). Interpreters identify her variously as a historical foreign princess, Israel as Yahweh's bride, or the church as Christ's bride (Eph 5:25-32). The allegory sustained centuries of Jewish and Christian exegesis.

ppl.name: The Sons of Korah
ppl.role: Korahite authors composing a royal wedding song (superscription)
ppl.text: The superscription calls this a shir yedidot — a 'song of loves' or wedding song. The poet's heart 'is stirred by a noble theme' and his tongue is 'the pen of a skillful writer' (v.1). This is the Psalter's most explicitly romantic composition, yet its placement in the canon invited theological reading beyond any particular royal marriage.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVAll your robes are fragrant with myrrh and aloes and cassia; from palaces adorned with ivory the music of the strings makes you glad.ESVyour robes are all fragrant with myrrh and aloes and cassia. From ivory palaces stringed instruments make you glad;NIVGird your sword on your side, you mighty one; clothe yourself with splendor and majesty.ESVGird your sword on your thigh, O mighty one, in your splendor and majesty!

rec.text: ""You are the most excellent of men and your lips have been anointed with grace." The ideal king: beautiful in speech, gracious in character."

rows.label: The Bride — 45:10–17
rows.text: "Forget your father’s house." All glorious within. Eternal memory.

hebtext.note: "Your throne (kisʾăk̅ā), O God, will last for ever and ever" (v.6). The most debated verse: is the king addressed AS God, or is "God" a parenthetical (your throne, O God, is forever)? Hebrews 1:8–9 applies it directly to Christ: "But about the Son he says: ‘Your throne, O God, will last for ever.’" A royal wedding psalm that becomes messianic: the king is anointed with the oil of joy (v.7), his robes are fragrant (v.8), the queen stands at his right hand in gold of Ophir (v.9).

"Listen, daughter, and pay attention: forget your people and your father’s house. Let the king be enthralled by your beauty" (vv.10–11). The bride is counselled to leave her old identity and enter the king’s household. The allegory: the church leaves the world to be enthralled by Christ. "The princess is all glorious within her chamber; her gown is interwoven with gold" (v.13). The psalm closes with a promise of lasting memory: "I will perpetuate your memory through all generations" (v.17).

themes.note: Prophecy peaks: Hebrews 1:8–9 applies the throne verse to Christ. Covenant: the wedding is covenantal. Worship: the bridal song.
